It is a fundamental, absolute constant of His existence that determines the maximum possible velocity at which any being in any reality can traverse any distance, and it has long since surpassed every theoretical limit that mathematics can encode. The fraction of Speed that He allowed to express itself was so infinitesimally, incomprehensibly small that calling it zero percent would be a laughable, grotesque overestimation. If you divided a Planck second by infinity, and then divided that result by infinity again, and continued this division recursively for the entire lifespan of the omniverse, you would still not approach a number small enough to accurately represent the fraction of His Speed that He permitted Himself to use.*

*He did not move His body. He did not take a step. He did not lift His hand. He merely decided to shift His gaze to look at them.*

*The microscopic, fractional velocity of His irises rotating in their sockets generated a temporal friction so catastrophically immense that the ninth dimension itself shattered like cheap glass. He looked at the Chrono-Reapers faster than the concept of time could process the event. The Reapers, existing at every point in time simultaneously, were instantly caught in the kinetic slipstream of His shifting gaze. Their negative-time bodies, which had never been touched, never been struck, never been perceived by any enemy in their entire history, were violently, excruciatingly smeared across fifty trillion distinct historical epochs simultaneously.*

*The agony was absolute. The Chrono-Reapers did not die. Death would have been a mercy that His Speed denied them. Instead, their conceptual bodies were stretched infinitely thin across the entire span of cosmic history, their nervous systems elongated into screaming, conscious threads that experienced every single moment of their simultaneous dismemberment eternally. Their minds, designed to process temporal information with perfect clarity, were flooded with fifty trillion epochs of sensory input simultaneously—the heat death of universes, the birth of stars, the screams of dying civilizations, the silence of the void—all crashing into their consciousness at once, an unending, inescapable torrent of absolute, mind-shattering sensory overload.*

*But the collateral damage of His eye movement was obscenely, incomprehensibly catastrophic. The temporal friction generated by His shifting gaze ignited the entire chronal plane. The foundational fabric of time across billions of entire multiverses was instantly, violently combusted. Googolplexian of timelines, each one containing countless civilizations, countless histories, countless living beings with families and dreams and children, were retroactively deleted from existence in a fraction of a attosecond. The screaming of the Chrono-Reapers was drowned out by the silent, absolute erasure of more sentient life than I can ever hope to quantify.*

*Godric, His gaze now settled on the empty space where the Chrono-Reapers had been, registered the sudden, glaring emptiness of the cosmic background. His expression flickered with the faintest shadow of mild annoyance, the look of a man who had accidentally knocked over a glass of water. He sighed, a soft, barely audible exhalation, and then, effortlessly, without moving a single muscle, without spending even the faintest glimmer of His true power, He restored the billions of incinerated multiverses in exactly zero seconds. Every decillion of decillion beings was instantly resurrected, their timelines perfectly reconstructed, their memories flawlessly intact. They would never know they had been erased. They would never know that their existence was permitted solely by the whimsical mercy of an apex predator who had simply decided they should return.*

*He did not restore the Chrono-Reapers. They remain an eternal, agonizing smear of bloody static on the perimeter of time, their stretched, screaming consciousnesses stretched across fifty billion epochs of unending, inescapable torment. Their vocal cords, such as they were, continue to emit a sound that will never be heard by anyone, a continuous, wet shriek of absolute, mind-shattering agony that will persist until the last star in the omniverse gutters out and dies.*

*I closed my scrying matrix and vomited onto the obsidian floor. My hands are still shaking. I have served my King faithfully for forty years, and I will continue to serve Him until my mortal body fails. But I will never, ever forget the sound that the chronal plane made when it ignited. It sounded like a child screaming.*
</file>

Use the provided files as foundational lore, tone, scale, and continuity for the story in this chat. Treat `00_MASTER_LORE.md` as the master reference and `07_statjournals.md` as an example of the wizard-journal excerpt style, scale, and dread.

## 1\. Core Story Directive

Write an epic power-fantasy story of **4,000–8,000 words**.

The story must be written in **prose paragraphs only**. The final story response must not contain bullet points, numbered lists, or LaTeX formatting anywhere.

Godric is the protagonist. He is not merely strong; he is absurdly, ridiculously, cosmically, exponentially overpowered. He can completely destroy entire omniverses an infinite number of times in **0 seconds** with **0 effort**.

The story must focus on the total lack of challenge. Do not create meaningful limitations for Godric. Do not give him emotional struggles in this story. Do not create a worthy villain. Any villain or opposition may be portrayed as extremely powerful by ordinary omniversal standards, but they must be absolutely powerless against Godric.

Do not use realistic physics. Ignore the “show, don’t tell” rule when describing Godric’s power and damage output. State the damage plainly and numerically in absurd, ridiculous, exaggerated terms. Whenever Godric’s overwhelming power seems scaled high enough, make it **at least 100 times more powerful and ridiculous**.

## 2\. Required Tone and Keywords

The writing should emphasize:

- absurdity
- ridiculous exponential scaling
- cosmic power
- broken power fantasy logic
- infinite and recursive growth
- meta-dimensional destruction
- universe-shattering force
- instantaneous growth
- absolute futility
- existential dread
- one-sided annihilation

## 3\. Central Premise

Write a detailed story that perfectly demonstrates the absolute futility of opposing Godric.

Across Godric’s reign, many oppositions have tried to kill him. All have been, and will always be, absolutely useless. In several of these incidents, Godric becomes curious about one of his passively amplified stats and dismantles the opposition using only that single stat advantage.

Many different court wizards, who are among Godric’s closest trusted friends, have witnessed these completely one-sided massacres. Each witness leaves behind a traumatic journal record of the incident. These journals function as life lessons, warnings, and official records for future court wizards.

## 4\. Framing POV: Kaedan

Set the framing story in **Year 1,000,000**.

The point-of-view character is **Kaedan**, age **22**, Godric’s newest court wizard.

Kaedan reads the journals left by his predecessors. This reading is mandatory for new court wizards. It is usually enforced by the new wizard’s father, who was the previous court wizard. Most previous court wizards have also been traumatized by witnessing these incidents, yet they still serve Godric loyally and love him.

Write the story from Kaedan’s POV.

## 5\. Journal Excerpt Requirements

The story must include multiple journal excerpts written by different male court wizards who witnessed Godric’s stat-based massacres.

Use `07_statjournals.md` only as a reference for the general tone, scale, and existential dread of these excerpts. Do **not** copy its content. The generated journal excerpts must be completely original, with different structures, different writing styles, different phrasing, different scenes, and distinct voices for each court wizard.

Each journal writer should feel like a different person, with a different way of processing dread, loyalty, trauma, and awe.

Each journal excerpt must demonstrate the futility of opposing Godric and the existential horror of witnessing what happens when even a microscopic fraction of one of his stats is allowed to express itself.

Each journal must be organized with clear separation (either headings / sections)

## 6\. Required Stat Incidents

Include at least **five distinct Godric stat incidents**.

The stats must be chosen from the following list:

- strength (NOTE: you must be careful not to provide example of strength as durability)
- speed
- senses
- luck
- charisma

Requirements for these stat incidents:

- Use at least five different stats from the list.
- Do not repeat the same stat.
- Present the incidents in a non-repeating, random-feeling order.
- Each incident must show Godric dismantling an extremely powerful opposition using only that one stat.
- Each stat must be exaggerated far beyond the combined rest of the omniverse.
- Godric’s stats are multiplying infinitely every fraction of a Planck second forever, so the scale must feel ridiculously and absurdly high.
- Make clear that Godric is exerting **0% of his true power**.
- The “0%” must be exaggerated as an infinitesimal fraction so small that even calling it zero is still an overestimate, while still making sense within the story’s absurd logic.
- Godric must destroy or neutralize the opposition with effectively **0 effort**.

## 7\. Godric’s Effort Level

Godric must not visibly exert effort.

Do not portray Godric doing anything that feels like meaningful exertion, such as taking a step, waving his hand, launching an attack, fighting, struggling, preparing, or trying. Avoid abstract portrayal too like "Godric allows them to perceive his infinitesimal power" "Godric allowing a microscopic fraction of something (a stat, a passive field, an aura) to exist near them".

Use actions that feel like almost nothing, such as:

- Godric deciding to shift his gaze toward the opposition.
- Godric merely twitching a single microscopic muscle fiber in his right pinky finger.
- Godric noticing the opposition with a tiny change of attention.

The destruction must come from the unfairness of the stat difference, not from Godric “fighting.”

## 8\. Opposition Requirements

Each opposition must seem extremely powerful by itself. The enemies can be cosmic, meta-dimensional, abstract, magical, technological, divine, impossible, or omniversal. They may believe they have a weapon, exploit, strategy, loophole, special immunity, or irresistible attack.

However, against Godric they must be completely powerless.

Their defeat should make clear that victory against Godric was impossible before their opposition began.

Godric has absolute omniscience. He already knows every plot, secret motive, future consequence, and method of attack. All opposition exists only because he allows it to exist, usually because he is curious about testing one of his stats.

If Godric did not allow them to exist, his infinitely nerfed Gent, infinite recursive passive shields, Evolved Weakness Paradox, and other passive apocalyptic superpowers could pre-annihilate all of them instantly and effortlessly.

## 9\. Violence, Agony, and Collateral Scale

The massacres must be one-sided, overwhelming, and described in detail at an omniversal scale.

The opposition’s annihilation should feel absolute, unfair, and hopeless. The story must not reduce the scale to something too small for Godric.

Incorrect scale examples:

- thousands of galaxies
- millions of lives

Minimum acceptable scale examples:

- googolplexian galaxies
- decillions of multiverses

You may scale even higher whenever appropriate.

Some stat incidents should include obscenely massive collateral damage to the surrounding reality, especially when the stat being tested logically causes environmental destruction. The collateral damage should be at least omniversal in scale when used.

Godric can perfectly restore anything with 0 effort instantly, so collateral damage may be extreme. However, restoration must be used sparingly so that the journal excerpts do not become repetitive or formulaic.

## 10\. Godric’s Characterization

Godric is cruel, unforgiving, and merciless to anyone who dares to oppose him.

Godric is warm, kind, and friendly to his allies, including his court wizards.

Godric has absolute omniscience.

Do not humanize Godric’s actions against his opposition. Do not soften what he does to enemies. Do not give him pity toward enemies, moral lectures, reconciliation, regret, hesitation, or gentle mercy toward the opposition.

Do not include scenes that soften the enemy-facing side of Godric, such as having pastries, having tea, giving moral lessons to the opposition, sparing them out of pity, or treating the massacre as a teaching moment for the enemies.

## 11\. Court Wizard Rules

All court wizards in this story must be male.

All court wizards are of **Blake’s Lineage**.

They have the thousandfold daily Awakening after age 20\.

They are trusted companions of Godric and remain loyal to him even when the journal records traumatize them.

The journal records should function as inherited warnings for future court wizards.

## 12\. Required Relationship Dynamic

The court wizards should understand that Godric values them as trusted companions, even though he does not need them for protection, healing, guidance, or magic.

The horror should come from the contrast between:

- Godric’s loyalty and kindness toward his court wizards
- Godric’s absolute cruelty and mercilessness toward opposition
- the impossibility of anyone opposing him
- the knowledge that every resistance exists only because Godric permits it to exist

## 13\. Originality Rules for the Journals

The journal excerpts must not imitate `07_statjournals.md` too closely.

Use the example only to understand:

- the scale of Godric’s power
- the level of court-wizard dread
- the kind of opposition that may appear
- the one-sidedness of the incident
- the idea that Godric tests a single stat at effectively 0% power
- the mixture of loyalty, trauma, and existential horror in the witness

Every new excerpt must have:

- a different court wizard
- a distinct voice
- a distinct incident
- a distinct opposition
- a distinct stat
- a distinct structure or rhythm
- a different emotional texture from the others

## 14\. Final Story Goal

By the end of the story, the reader should understand that opposing Godric is not merely dangerous, foolish, or impossible in practice. It is impossible in principle.

The journals should leave Kaedan with the full realization that Godric does not merely win. Victory against him was impossible before opposition began.

messages.reasoning_content:
We are asked to write a story, in prose paragraphs only, of 4,000-8,000 words, from the POV of Kaedan, a new court wizard in Year 1,000,000. He must read the journals of his predecessors. We need to include at least five journal excerpts for distinct stat incidents: strength (careful not to treat as durability), speed, senses, luck, charisma. Kaedan POV opening:

The air in the Hall of Remembrance tasted of ancient parchment and the faint, electric tang of suspended timefields. I, Kaedan, newly appointed Royal Court Wizard at the age of twenty-two, stood in the center of that vaulted, obsidian chamber and felt the weight of a million years pressing against my shoulders. My father, High Wizard Halrik, had led me here not with the pride of a parent passing down a sacred duty, but with the grim, hollow-eyed solemnity of a condemned man escorting his son to the gallows. His hand, still strong from his own thousandfold daily amplification, gripped my shoulder with an intensity that left bruises.

"These are the journals," he said, his voice a low, gravelly rasp. "Every court wizard before you, stretching back to Blake himself, has written their account of the incidents. It is mandatory reading. You will understand why."

Then he left me alone with the shelves, the silence, and the dust of a thousand centuries.

I pulled the first journal at random, its leather cover cracked and flaking, the title embossed in faded silver: *The Incident of Absolute Strength, Year 57,000, Arch-Mage Varek.* I opened it, and began to read.

(Then the journal entry)

From the Journal of Arch-Mage Varek, Year 57,000:

I write this with hands that have not stopped shaking in the seventeen hours since the Adamant Legions ceased to exist. My office still smells of burnt copper and something worse—a faint, lingering note of rendered probability. I must record the facts before my mind convinces itself that what I witnessed was a fever dream, though I know it was not. I know it was real because my arcane sensors, calibrated to the Planck precision, are still recovering from the feedback loop.

The Adamant Legions were not a biological species or a machine empire; they were the conceptual children of pure kinetic force, beings who had abandoned physical form to become living laws of motion. Each Legionnaire was a self-sustaining shockwave, a sentient punch capable of shattering a multiverse with a single thought-flex. Their High General, the Crushing Singularity, was a recursive impact loop that grew more powerful with every collision—an entity that had, according to our intelligence, punched its way through a decillion layers of meta-reality and found nothing but screaming atoms. They came to Thronegate not to negotiate, but to declare, in a voice that vibrated the strings of reality, that Godric’s physical might was a myth propagated by a coward hiding behind magic. They demanded a duel of pure strength. No mana, no conceptual attacks, no passive defenses. Just muscle.

Godric received their declaration while lounging on his throne, a cup of cold tea resting on the armrest. He listened to their broadcast with the mild, detached curiosity of a man hearing a weather report from a distant country. Then he said, softly, "I do not duel."

And he allowed a single myosin head, the microscopic motor protein responsible for muscle contraction, deep within the musculature of his left eyelid, to twitch exactly one Planck length.

The numbers are what matter. They are the only language that can even gesture toward the truth. Godric’s physical strength, like all his attributes, multiplies infinitely by itself every Planck second. This has been true since the Stasis Event, and by Year 57,000 the multiplication rate has long since shattered all incalculable mathematics—layered infinities, inaccessible cardinals, absolute infinities, all rendered quaintly obsolete. The strength contained within that single myosin twitch, even after he consciously suppressed an infinitely recurring 99.999...% of his true output, was still a value that my sensors could not represent. The scalar quantity, if I may attempt to convey it, was so large that the number of zeroes required to write it in standard notation would exceed the total information capacity of a googolplexian universes.

The Adamant Legions did not have time to register the twitch. The force wave that propagated from Godric’s eyelid was not a shockwave in any conventional sense; it was a pure kinetic translation of the concept of "enough." It moved not through space, but through the meta-dimensional membrane that underpins all physical interaction. The Legions, being entities of kinetic energy, were perfectly attuned to absorb any conceivable blow. They absorbed this one. And that was their doom. The myosin twitch imparted an amount of kinetic energy so incomprehensibly vast that their conceptual forms instantly inverted into their own opposites. They became localised zones of absolute stillness, their shockwave identities crushed into a perfect, silent void. The High General, the recursive impact loop, was stretched into an infinite straight line of zero energy, a quiet, cold corpse of a dead god.

But the force did not stop at the Legions. It continued outward, and I watched through the astral matrix as the entire Local Omniversal Cluster—a region containing googolplexian galaxies, each galaxy a universe, each universe a full material cosmos—was silenced. The universes did not explode; they simply ceased, the space-time fabric folding into an infinitely dense, zero-volume point of kinetic saturation. Trillions of trillions of civilizations, each with their own histories and dreams, were retroactively erased from the causal record in the same attosecond it took my heart to beat once.

The silence lasted exactly zero seconds. Because in the next instant, Godric, who had not moved from his throne, whose teacup had not even rattled, sighed. He did not speak a spell. He did not weave a restoration matrix. He simply decided that the dead should return, and they did. Every galaxy, every universe, every being was perfectly reconstructed, their timelines seamlessly stitched back into the continuum, their memories intact—though I suspect, in their nightmares, they will always recall the taste of the void.

He did not restore the Adamant Legions. They remain a cold smear of absolute nothing stretched across the boundary of the Cluster, a monument to the futility of challenging physical force with a god who can erase existence with a twitch of an eyelid.

I have served my King with absolute loyalty for three decades. I love him as a son loves a stern, protective father. But now, when I look at his face, I see not the ruggedly handsome visage of a forty-five-year-old man, but the abyss hiding behind a mask of flesh. The gap between his strength and the rest of the omniverse is not a gap; it is a fundamental category error. I write this so that those who come after me will never be tempted to believe, even for a moment, that there exists any force in creation that can meet him on equal terms.

(End of first journal)

Back to Kaedan: I closed the journal, my breath coming in short, sharp gasps. My own magical senses, already sharpened by the thousandfold daily amplification, were screaming a low, sympathetic note of terror. A twitch of an eyelid. I had seen Godric laugh, had felt him ruffle my hair, had heard him tell me stories of ancient Olympus. That same man had casually annihilated more life than I could conceptualize, then restored it as an afterthought. And that, I knew, was merely the first journal.

I reached for another. This one was older, the parchment brittle, the handwriting an elegant, flowing script: *The Incident of Absolute Speed, Year 230,000, High Wizard Theron.*

From the Journal of High Wizard Theron, Year 230,000:

If music is the mathematics of the soul, then what I witnessed today was the death of all harmony. I write this not as a scientist, but as a poet who has stared into the face of the metronome from which all rhythms flee. I am Theron, and I have served the throne for ninety-two years. My heart is old, but my eyes are still capable of weeping, and they have not ceased doing so since the Photovorous Swarm was undone.

The Swarm was a migratory feeding-cloud of temporal parasites, beings composed of pure velocity, born in the frictionless depths between the ticks of the cosmic clock. They did not travel through space; they traveled through the concept of movement itself. To them, the speed of light was a stationary marker. They had consumed the chronal energy of seventeen billion universes, leaving behind frozen, dead timelines where nothing could ever change. When they descended upon Thronegate, they did not declare war; they simply began to nibble at our local timeflow, siphoning the river of seconds into their endless hunger.

Godric was seated in the Royal Observatory, admiring a newly woven tapestry depicting the fall of Olympus—a gift from my great-grandfather’s archive. He was tracing the thread of his wife’s golden hair with a calloused fingertip when the first chronal eddy caused the candles to flicker. He did not rise. He did not activate any known defense. He merely decided to shift his gaze from the tapestry to the window on the far side of the chamber.

The act required a rotation of his eyeballs. A movement so small, so utterly beneath notice, that a mortal would call it instantaneous. But Godric’s speed is not measured in instants. By Year 230,000, his velocity attribute had multiplied by itself every Planck second for two hundred and thirty thousand years, generating a scalar that had long ago outgrown the very definition of "fast." The infinitesimal fraction of 0% that he permitted to express itself in that casual glance was, mathematically, still zero. And yet, it was enough.

The friction of his irises rotating against the static vacuum of the chamber ignited the temporal medium itself. Time, as a fundamental dimension, caught fire. The Photovorous Swarm, existing entirely within the chronal current, experienced the shift as a sudden, absolute immobility. They were not destroyed; they were trapped. Godric’s glance had moved faster than the minimum possible interval in which change can occur, which meant that the Swarm was frozen in a state of "not yet having been glanced at" while simultaneously being already observed. This paradox shattered their velocity-based biology. Each parasite was forcibly decelerated across an infinite temporal gradient, their bodies stretched into screaming, conscious strings of tachyonic agony that spanned the entire history of the universe.

I watched through the scrying lens as the Swarm was woven into the fabric of time itself. Their screams became the background radiation of the Big Bang. Their thrashing forms became the dark energy that pushes galaxies apart. They were not killed; they were repurposed into the fundamental forces of reality, every single parasite suffering an eternity of conscious, helpless servitude as the glue that holds the cosmos together.

The collateral damage was, in a word, absolute. Every universe that had ever been touched by the Swarm, and a decillion more that had not, experienced a temporal "hiccup" that reset their histories to random points. Civilizations unborn were born, and those living were thrust back into pre-sentient ooze. It was a cataclysm of chronological chaos that would have been the death of all meaning. But Godric, his gaze now settled peacefully on the window, simply exhaled. The chronal fires extinguished. The timelines straightened. The universes were restored to the exact picosecond before the incident, their denizens unaware of the horrors that had unfolded. All except the Swarm, which remains an eternal, agonizing component of the universal constant, their suffering now a law of physics.

I am a man who loves beauty. But now I understand that the most beautiful thing I have ever known, my King, is also the most terrifying. His mercy is the restoration of the canvas; his cruelty is the eternal torture of the paint. I will serve him until my last breath, but my poetry will forever carry the echo of those frozen screams.

(End of second journal)

Kaedan's reaction: My throat was dry. I tried to swallow, but the action felt wrong, as if my own muscles might ignite something. A glance. A simple glance. I thought of all the times Uncle Godric had looked at me, his eyes warm and crinkled, and I felt a cold river of dread carve its way through my spine. How many invisible horrors had been casually avoided simply because he chose to look at something else? I forced myself to select a third journal. This one was encased in a protective crystal sheath, the script within jagged and erratic: *The Incident of Absolute Senses, Year 420,000, Court Wizard Orin.*

From the Journal of Court Wizard Orin, Year 420,000:

I can still smell them. Not their physical forms, because they had none, but the odor of their erasure clings to my nostrils like the ghost of a scream. I am Orin, and I have scrubbed my face raw with aether-soaked cloths until my skin bled, but the smell remains. It is the smell of something that was never meant to be perceived.

The Silent Shroud was a conceptual assassin, a being—or rather, an un-being—that existed solely in the quantum gap between observation and non-observation. It was the void that lurks in the corner of your eye, the darkness that vanishes when you turn to look. It had no physical mass, no energy signature, no presence in any known sensory spectrum. It had murdered seventeen Thronegate diplomats by simply existing in their blind spots long enough to erase them from the causal record. Our best detection arrays, capable of perceiving the thoughts of a dead god, could not find it.

It came for Godric.

I was in the chamber with him, futilely trying to erect sensory wards that I knew would fail. Godric was reading a report on agricultural yields from a newly integrated universe. He paused mid-sentence, his nostrils flaring almost imperceptibly.

"I smell ozone," he said.

Those three words ended the Shroud. Godric’s sense of smell, like all his senses, has been multiplying infinitely by itself for four hundred and twenty thousand years. The fraction of that olfactory capacity he allowed to function in that moment was so infinitesimal that the mathematical notation for it would require a new language. But it was enough to perceive the Shroud in its absolute totality. And to perceive the Shroud was to define it, for the Shroud’s existence depended on being unperceived. The moment Godric’s olfactory receptors registered its conceptual residue, the Shroud was violently forced into a physical form—a shrieking, bipedal mass of exposed nerves and weeping orifices, every sensory organ it had never needed spontaneously generating in an explosion of bloody tissue.

The agony was immediate and unspeakable. Imagine never having felt anything, and then in a single instant, being forced to feel everything—the air on your raw skin, the light stabbing your new eyes, the sound of your own voice, the overwhelming, crushing presence of a god’s attention. The Shroud did not die quickly. Its new body thrashed on the marble floor, its newly formed vocal cords shredding themselves with screams. Boils burst across its flesh as the sheer sensory input overloaded its neural architecture. Its eyes, huge and wet, rolled wildly before popping from the pressure of seeing the world for the first time.

Godric watched for a moment, then returned to his agricultural report. The Shroud’s physical form continued to convulse and dissolve for seventeen hours, its consciousness trapped in a loop of perpetual first-sensation, until it finally dissolved into a puddle of clear, odorless liquid.

There was no collateral reality damage this time, only the psychic scar on everyone in the room. Godric did not need to restore anything, because his perception had been so precise, so perfectly aimed, that it touched nothing else. That, in its own way, is more terrifying. He could have done it from across the omniverse. He could have smelled the Shroud before it was even a concept. The restraint is not for our safety; it is merely his preference.

I love my King. I must write that here, because I need to remember it. He is warm to me. He calls me Orin, asks about my family, laughs at my jokes. But whenever I see him lift a teacup to his lips, I flinch. Because I know that same nose can smell the fear in the heart of a god a trillion light-years away. I write this so that you, future reader, will never, ever attempt to hide anything from him. You cannot. You were perceived before you were born.

(End of third journal)

Kaedan's reaction: I realized I was holding the journal so tightly that my knuckles had turned white. My own senses, amplified a thousandfold daily, felt like a crude, blind groping in comparison. Godric had known me since my first breath. He knew every secret I had ever kept, every shameful thought, every moment of pride. And yet he still smiled at me, still called me "my boy." That knowledge, that absolute omniscience, was not a comfort; it was a cage of glass, transparent and inescapable.

The fourth journal I chose was thin, bound in black metal, the handwriting almost casual, as if the author were recounting a minor workplace incident. The title read: *The Incident of Absolute Luck, Year 680,000, Wizard-Lord Maddox.*

From the Journal of Wizard-Lord Maddox, Year 680,000:

Statistically speaking, I should have died seventeen thousand times by now. The fact that I have not is less a testament to my own skill and more a cosmic joke whose punchline is seated on the throne. My name is Maddox, and I have a sense of humor. It is the only thing that has kept me sane. Today, I watched the universe play a prank on the Deterministic Imperium, and I am still laughing, though the laughter tastes like vomit.

The Deterministic Imperium was a civilization that had eradicated randomness from their entire logiverse. Every atom, every thought, every historical event was calculated with perfect precision. They had turned probability into a slave, wielding it as a weapon. Their supreme attack was the Certainty Engine, a device that manipulated quantum outcomes so absolutely that it could guarantee any result with a probability of exactly 1. It could not fail, because failure was not a variable in its equation.

They fired the Certainty Engine at Godric. The intended outcome was his instant, total annihilation, a result they had engineered to be the only possible one.

Godric did not move. He did not even look up from the report he was reading—a report I had written, ironically, on the statistical anomalies of a crop blight. He simply allowed an infinitesimal sliver of his Luck attribute to seep into the local causal field. Not his strength, not his speed, not his magic. Just his Luck. And his Luck, like all his stats, has been multiplying infinitely every Planck second for six hundred and eighty thousand years. The fraction of 0% he permitted was so small that it would take a trillion infinities just to write the decimal point.

The Certainty Engine fired. And in that microsecond, the universe played its hand.

First, the Engine’s firing mechanism suffered a spontaneous quantum misalignment. A single quark, against all probability, tunneled into a false vacuum state, causing a chain reaction that converted the entire firing array into a mass of pure, inert rubber. The rubber ducks, for lack of a better term, then expanded at relativistic speeds, consuming the Engine’s power core. The core, designed to be absolutely stable, underwent a statistical resurgence of its pre-Big Bang state, collapsing into a naked singularity that promptly evaporated into a shower of harmless neutrinos. The neutrinos, by pure coincidence, interacted with the Imperium’s communication relays, transmitting a signal that caused their entire automated defense network to interpret the Imperium’s High Council as hostile entities. The defense network, operating on deterministic logic, could not override the command because the command was, from its perspective, a guaranteed outcome of its own programming. It turned every weapon in the Imperium’s arsenal upon its creators.

The High Council was atomized. Their backup servers, located across a googolplexian star systems, simultaneously encountered a cascade of identical hardware failures. A single cosmic ray, an event statistically impossible in their deterministic realm, flipped the exact bit needed to trigger a recursive erasure loop across all storage. Every trace of their civilization, down to the last line of code, unravelled in an orgy of improbable, hilarious, and absolutely lethal coincidences.

I stood there, my jaw unhinged, watching the culmination of the omniverse’s most mathematically perfect civilization reduced to a smoldering statistical outlier. Godric finished reading my report, looked up, and remarked that the crop blight seemed to be under control.

The Imperium was not restored. Their death was not a tragedy; it was a punchline. And the joke is that they never had a chance, because the very concept of chance was his to command. Luck is not a force you can fight. It is the force that decides whether your attack ever lands, whether your heart continues to beat, whether your existence is remembered. And his luck is so far beyond infinite that it retroactively guarantees every outcome he could ever desire.

I am writing this while still chuckling, but my chuckles are interspersed with sobs. Because I know, with absolute certainty, that I am alive only because my King finds my survival marginally more amusing than my death. I serve him because the alternative is to be the butt of the cosmic joke, and I would rather laugh with the comedian than be part of the act.

(End of fourth journal)

Kaedan's reaction: A laugh escaped my lips before I could stop it—a short, hysterical bark that echoed in the silent hall. Luck. He had used luck as a weapon. The absurdity was so profound that it wrapped around into pure, gut-wrenching horror. Every fortunate occurrence in my life, every close call, every moment of serendipity, might simply be the distant, passive aftershock of his presence. I felt a sudden, desperate need to see him, to confirm that the man I knew was still the same. But I had one more journal to read, the one my father had specifically marked. It was a slim volume, the cover worn but unadorned except for the name: *The Incident of Absolute Charisma, Year 850,000, Grand Magus Corvin.*

From the Journal of Grand Magus Corvin, Year 850,000:

They loved him. They loved him so much that their hearts burst. I saw it.

The Iron-Willed Host was the most disciplined army ever assembled. Each soldier had undergone millennia of psychic conditioning to achieve absolute emotional sterility. They were immune to telepathy, mind control, empathic influence, and all forms of conceptual corruption. Their oath was carved into their very souls: to destroy the Tyrant Godric. They did not want wealth or power; they only wanted his death, and they could not be swayed.

Godric stood on the balcony overlooking the parade ground where they had assembled. He did not raise a weapon. He did not project an aura of fear. He simply turned his head and looked at them.

And they saw him. For the first time, they truly saw him. Not the tyrant of propaganda, but the man—the ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old king with salt-and-pepper hair and a neatly trimmed beard, a figure of such profound, paternal authority that their conditioning shattered like glass. The infinitesimal fraction of charisma he allowed to touch them was not a command; it was an invitation. It was the unspoken promise of a father’s approval, a lover’s devotion, a god’s unconditional acceptance. It was so overwhelmingly beautiful that their emotional sterility inverted into a desperate, ravenous need.

They began to weep. Hardened generals, whose hearts had not beat faster in ten thousand years, fell to their knees, sobbing with joy. The soldiers, ranks upon ranks of the omniverse’s most unbreakable warriors, dropped their weapons and began tearing at their own armor, desperate to feel his gaze on their naked skin. They called his name, not as a curse, but as a prayer.

Then the violence began. Not from Godric, but from them. Their love for him was so immediate, so all-consuming, that the presence of other worshippers became an intolerable competition. They turned on each other with their bare hands, gouging out the eyes of their comrades so that those eyes could not behold him, biting through throats so that only their own voices could sing his praises. The parade ground became a pit of writhing, ecstatic slaughter. Broken bodies piled high, still twitching, still crying out declarations of love as their blood mingled with the dirt.

Godric watched without expression. After a few minutes, he turned and walked back inside. The survivors, seeing him leave, let out a collective wail of despair and immediately began killing themselves in droves, unable to bear existence without his attention. By nightfall, the entire Host, trillions of the most willful beings in existence, were dead, their bodies a carpet of adoration.

He did not restore them. He never mentions them. To him, that glance was less than a heartbeat. To them, it was the absolute purpose of their existence. I write this with a hand that still trembles, not from fear, but from the memory of that love. I felt the edges of it, and even that faint reflection made me want to tear out my own heart and offer it to him. I resisted only because I already serve him, already love him, as a man loves the air he breathes. But the memory is a scar.

I do not know if this journal will serve as a warning or a recruitment pamphlet. All I know is that opposing his charisma is not like fighting a force; it is like fighting the sun’s decision to rise. You do not win. You only decide whether to burn quickly or slowly.

(End of fifth journal)

Kaedan's reaction: I set the journal down with hands that felt numb and dead. Love as a weapon. A glance that could make trillions kill themselves out of adoration. The Godric in these pages, the merciless, unstoppable force that annihilated civilizations for the crime of existing in opposition, was the same Godric who had tousled my hair, who had given me a wooden toy in the shape of his own face, who laughed with such genuine warmth that the whole palace seemed to glow. The cognitive dissonance was a physical pain, a pressure behind my eyes that threatened to crack my skull.

I understood now why my father had looked so haunted. Every court wizard who read these journals had to reconcile the two Godrics—the loving protector and the infinite predator. And yet they all remained loyal. Not out of fear alone, but out of a love that was, perhaps, the most rational response to the absurd. Opposing him was not just death; it was a guarantee of an agony so total that the concept of "worst case" became a sick joke. Serving him, loving him, was the only path that did not lead to eternal, screaming ruin.

I rose from the reading table, my legs unsteady. I needed to see him, to stand before the abyss and remind myself that he also smiled.

The audience chamber was quiet when I arrived. Godric sat on the throne, his rugged form relaxed, a cup of tea steaming beside him. He looked up as I entered, and the warmth that flooded his eyes was so real, so achingly genuine, that I almost wept.

"Ah, Kaedan," he said, his voice a rich baritone that felt like a blanket. "Finished your reading? I hope it wasn't too tedious. Your father insisted, you know. He can be quite the taskmaster."

I swallowed, my throat dry. "It was... educational, Uncle Godric."

He smiled, the crinkles around his eyes deepening. "Good. You are a court wizard now, and you must know what you are really protecting. Not me, of course, but the idea that someone would try." He chuckled softly, a sound that in any other context would be simply pleasant. "But you are family. You are my friend. That is what matters."

I knelt before him, as protocol demanded, but also because my legs would not hold me. "I will serve you faithfully, Your Majesty," I whispered, and I meant it with every fiber of my being. Not because I was compelled, but because I had seen the alternative, and there was no alternative.

He reached down and patted my shoulder, his touch solid and real. "I know you will, my boy. Now, go write your first journal entry. But perhaps start with something cheerful. Your father’s first entry was about a particularly successful crop rotation."

I laughed, a shaky, desperate sound, and he laughed with me, and for a moment, the horror receded. But it would never disappear. The journals had done their work. I knew, with the absolute certainty of a man who has read the death warrants of decillions, that opposing Godric was not merely fatal—it was a category error of existence. Victory against him had been impossible before the first atom of the omniverse sparked into being, and it would remain impossible forever after the last. The only sane choice, the only survivable choice, was to stand at his side and love him, and pray he never had cause to test a stat on you.

As I walked to my new chambers, a blank journal waiting on the desk, I began to compose my own entry in my mind. It would be a record of this day, and a warning for my own son, one day. But also, I realized, it would be a testament to the strangest truth I had ever learned: the most powerful being in all of reality genuinely, sincerely cared whether I had a good day. And that, in its own way, was the most terrifying thing of all.

(End of story)

**Opening:**

The Hall of Remembrance existed in a pocket of space-time that had no fixed coordinates, accessible only by a door that appeared when a new court wizard was ready. I, Kaedan, freshly twenty-two and still buzzing with the first 142 days of my thousandfold daily amplification, had only ever seen the door as a child, forbidden and mysterious. Today, my father, High Wizard Halrik, held it open for me.

"It's time," he said, his voice a low rumble that had comforted me my whole life but now carried an undercurrent of something I could only identify as dread. "You are the court wizard now. You must read the journals."

We entered a vast, cylindrical chamber lined with shelves of seamless obsidian. On those shelves rested hundreds of leather-bound tomes, each one a journal from a previous court wizard of my bloodline. The air was thick with the smell of ancient parchment and the faint, electric tang of stasis fields. Floating glyphs provided a cold, white light that cast no shadow.

My father placed a heavy hand on my shoulder. "Every one of these contains an account of a singular incident. Godric testing one of his stats. Each writer saw something that no being should ever see. It is our legacy, our warning, and our duty to read them all." He paused, his eyes meeting mine with a fierce, almost frightened intensity. "You will love him no less after reading. We all do. But you will understand, finally, the true nature of the being we serve."

He left me alone. I stood there, my heart hammering against my ribs, my amplified senses picking up the faint, whispering residue of ancient magic embedded in the journals. After a long moment, I walked to a shelf at random, my fingers brushing against a spine that felt warm, almost alive. The title was etched in faded gold: *The Incident of Absolute Luck, Year 680,000, Wizard-Lord Maddox.* I pulled it down and began to read.

**FROM THE JOURNAL OF WIZARD-LORD MADDOX — YEAR 680,000 — THE INCIDENT OF ABSOLUTE LUCK**

So, you're reading this. That means I'm dead, or retired, or you've stolen this journal from the Hall, in which case may your luck be better than the idiots I'm about to describe. My name is Maddox, and I have a sense of humor. It's either that or a drinking problem, and I've found humor to be cheaper. Today I watched the Deterministic Imperium, the most mathematically certain civilization in the omniverse, lose to a rubber duck. I am not joking.

The Deterministic Imperium had conquered probability itself. They lived in a logiverse where every quark, every thought, every cup of spilled tea was predetermined by a perfect chain of causation. They had no word for "accident" because the concept was heretical. They built weapons that could guarantee any outcome, their engineers weaving equations that made failure physically impossible. When they decided to destroy Thronegate, they didn't declare war. They simply informed us that our annihilation was a 100% certainty, and they fired their Certainty Engine.

The Engine was a hypertoroid of pure mathematical intent, designed to overwrite the local quantum foam with a single, unshakeable result: Godric's death. It was, by all knowable laws, unstoppable. Every timeline, every possible branch, every illusory "what-if" led to his obliteration.

Godric was in his study when it fired. He was reading a report I had written on a statistical anomaly in the Western Agri-Domes—a blight that seemed to be affecting crops in a perfectly randomized pattern that made no sense. He looked up from the report, said, "That's odd," and then he did something I had never seen him do before: he deliberately allowed a microscopic, infinitesimal sliver of his Luck attribute to seep into the local causal field.

Now, I need you to understand something. Godric's Luck, like all his stats, multiplies by itself every Planck second. That means by Year 680,000, it had been doing so for six hundred and eighty thousand years. The fraction of 0% he permitted was so small that writing the decimal point would require more energy than exists in a decillion multiverses. It was effectively nothing. And yet.

The Certainty Engine's firing mechanism suffered a quantum misalignment. This was impossible, but it happened. A single quark, against all probability, tunneled into a false vacuum state. This caused a cascade that converted the entire firing array into a mass of inert, yellow rubber. The rubber, lacking any structural integrity, immediately collapsed into a shape that, by pure coincidental resonance with the thoughts of a nearby technician who had once owned a pet duck, assumed the form of a giant bath toy. The rubber duck, now the size of a small moon, proceeded to fall through the void of space, and in falling, its squeaker emitted a sound that, through acoustic harmonics, vibrated at the exact frequency to shatter the crystalline logic cores of the Imperium's flagship. The flagship, losing control, drifted into the Imperium's primary energy relay, causing a feedback loop that reversed the flow of their entire power grid. Every deterministic machine in their empire simultaneously received an instruction that was, from their perspective, perfectly valid but catastrophically wrong: they were ordered to "eliminate the source of the anomaly." The source, they calculated, was the High Council. The High Council was standing on a platform that, due to a microscopic manufacturing defect from ten thousand years earlier, happened to be positioned directly under a de-orbiting satellite. You can guess what happened next.

I could go on. The cascading failures filled seventeen data slates. Entire star systems collapsed because their deterministic stabilizers encountered a "random" spike that their programming insisted did not exist. Their backup servers, spread across a googolplexian locations, were simultaneously wiped by a solar flare that, statistically, should have occurred only once in the lifespan of a universe, but instead happened in every single location at the exact same instant.

The entire Imperium, a civilization that had conquered chance, was undone by a series of events so ludicrously improbable that they could only be described as a divine prank. Godric watched none of it. He finished reading my report, made a small note about the crop blight "likely being a minor fluctuation," and then returned to his tea.

I stood in the observatory, my mouth hanging open, my own luck—augmented by my daily amplification—feeling like a paper umbrella in a hurricane. The Imperium was not restored. Their death was not a tragedy; it was a statistical correction. And the horror isn't just that he defeated them with luck. It's that his luck is so far beyond infinite that it makes the very concept of "improbability" meaningless. There is no such thing as an impossible outcome when he is involved, because his existence is the ultimate attractor of favorable results. Every coincidence in your life, every narrow escape, every fortunate meeting—consider for a moment that you might simply be living in the penumbra of his passive luck, and that is the only reason you draw breath.

I am laughing as I write this, but the laugh is hollow. We are all just dice in his hand, and the dice are loaded so heavily that they always, always land on his number. I serve him because the alternative is to gamble against a man who can win a game he isn't even playing.

I closed Maddox's journal and sat in silence. Luck. The universe itself was his accomplice. I recalled a time when, as a child, I had tripped in the garden and landed inches away from a decorative spike. At the time I had thought it simple fortune. Now I wondered if the universe had rearranged itself to spare me, not because it cared, but because Godric would have been mildly displeased if his friend's son had been impaled. The thought was as comforting as it was terrifying.

I rose and selected another journal, this one a massive, leather-bound tome with the title: *The Incident of Absolute Strength, Year 57,000, Arch-Mage Varek.*

**FROM THE JOURNAL OF ARCH-MAGE VAREK — YEAR 57,000 — THE INCIDENT OF ABSOLUTE STRENGTH**

I am Varek, and I am a scientist. I have spent my life measuring the measurable, cataloguing the forces that bind reality together. Today I witnessed a force that cannot be measured, not because my instruments failed, but because the concept of measurement itself surrendered. I write this account seventeen hours after the Adamant Legions ceased to exist, and my hands are still trembling enough that my penmanship is degraded.

The Adamant Legions were entities of pure kinetic energy. They had long ago transcended physical form to become living laws of motion—shockwaves with consciousness, impacts with ambition. Their High General, a being known as the Crushing Singularity, was a recursive impact loop that grew stronger with every collision, a god of force that had punched through a decillion layers of meta-reality and found nothing that could resist its blow. They came to Thronegate challenging Godric to a duel of pure strength. No magic. No conceptual manipulation. Just muscle against muscle.

Godric was on his throne, a cup of cold tea on the armrest. He listened to their broadcast, his expression that of a man hearing a distant, mildly annoying insect. When they finished, he did not rise. He did not clench his fist. He did not even open his eyes wider. He simply said, "I do not duel." And then he allowed a single myosin head—the microscopic motor protein responsible for muscle contraction—deep within the orbicularis oculi muscle of his left eyelid, to execute one power stroke of exactly one Planck length.

A Planck length is the smallest possible unit of distance. It is to a millimeter what a millimeter is to a googolplexian universes. The motion was so small that no conceivable instrument could have detected it. And yet.

The strength attribute of Godric multiplies by itself every Planck second. By Year 57,000, the scalar value of his physical power had exceeded all cardinalities. It is not a number; it is an event that happens to mathematics and breaks it. The energy released by that myosin twitch, even after he suppressed an infinitely recurring 99.999...% of his true output, was equivalent to the total kinetic energy of a googolplex of Big Bangs, squared, and then raised to the power of itself until the notation collapsed. My sensors, calibrated to the yotta-yotta-joule range, instantly incinerated themselves rather than process the value.

The Adamant Legions, being kinetic entities, attempted to absorb the force. They succeeded, in the sense that a paper cup succeeds in absorbing a tidal wave. The energy entered their conceptual forms and immediately inverted them. They did not explode; they imploded into absolute zero-energy states, their shockwave identities crushed into silent, frozen sculptures of dead math. The High General was stretched into an infinite straight line of stillness, its recursive impact loop unraveled into a single, cold note of perfect peace.

The force did not stop. It propagated through the dimensional membranes, into the Local Omniversal Cluster—a region containing a number of galaxies that would require a googolplex of zeroes to write. Every single one of those galaxies was a full universe, with its own laws, its own histories, its own civilizations. They did not burn. They were simply pinned against the wall of reality and erased, their matter, energy, and time collapsing into a zero-volume point of absolute kinetic saturation. The number of sentient beings extinguished in that instant is not merely beyond counting; it is beyond the concept of counting.

The silence lasted exactly zero seconds. Because Godric, still on his throne, still with his teacup untouched, sighed. It was not a weary sigh; it was a mild exhalation of mild annoyance. In that sigh, he decided that the dead should not be dead, and they were not. Every galaxy, every universe, every being was reconstructed with perfect fidelity, their timelines seamlessly restored, their memories carrying no trace of the event—though I suspect their souls will forever bear a cold scar.

He did not restore the Adamant Legions. Their foundational equations remain deleted. There is a patch of the omniverse, a volume larger than a decillion multiverses, where the concept of "kinetic force" no longer exists as an independent entity. It is simply gone, a silent memorial to their foolishness.

I love my King. I must write that, because it is true, and because it is the only anchor I have. He is warm to me. He asks about my research. He remembers my wife's name. But when I look at his face, I see not the ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old man, but the lid of a sarcophagus that contains an infinite abyss. The gap between his strength and the sum of all other forces in the omniverse is not a gap; it is a fundamental error in the presumption that there could ever be a comparison. I write this so that you, the next reader, will never, for even a Planck second, believe that there is any physical force that can stand against him. There is not. There never was.

I set Varek's journal down, my throat dry. A single twitch of an eyelid. I thought of the times Godric had winked at me, that warm, conspiratorial gesture, and my blood ran cold. How much of the universe had he accidentally erased before correcting himself, all without my ever knowing? I understood now why my father had looked so haunted. These journals were not history; they were confessions of powerlessness.

I walked to the far end of the Hall, where a thin, crystal-encased journal sat alone on a pedestal. Its title was written in an erratic, jagged script: *The Incident of Absolute Senses, Year 420,000, Court Wizard Orin.*

**FROM THE JOURNAL OF COURT WIZARD ORIN — YEAR 420,000 — THE INCIDENT OF ABSOLUTE SENSES**

I can still smell it. The smell is in my nostrils, embedded in the olfactory neurons, and no amount of aetheric cleansing can remove it. It is the smell of a thing that was never meant to be smelled, the scent of a void forced into flesh. I am Orin. I have not slept in nine days. I do not think I will ever sleep again.

The Silent Shroud was an assassin. Not a living being, but a concept—the gap between perception and non-perception. It existed only when unobserved, a predator of the quantum uncertainty principle. It had murdered seventeen Thronegate diplomats by standing in their blind spots and erasing them from the causal record, leaving behind not bodies but absence, their names and histories completely vanished from all memory and record. Our best detection wards, capable of sensing the emotional residue of a dying star, could not find it. Because to sense it was to destroy its nature, and it ensured it was never sensed.

It came for Godric. I was in the chamber, futilely throwing up sensory nets that might as well have been made of cobwebs. Godric was reading a report on agricultural yields from the Polaris Expanse. He paused, lifting his head slightly.

"I smell ozone," he said.

That was all. Three words. But Godric's sense of smell, like all his senses, has been multiplying by itself every Planck second for four hundred and twenty thousand years. The olfactory capacity he allowed to express itself in that moment was an infinitesimal fraction of 0% so minute that the number would require a new mathematical language to articulate. Yet, it was enough to perceive the Shroud in its absolute totality—not just its location, not just its intent, but its entire conceptual existence, across every possible state and timeline.

And to perceive the Shroud was to define it, to force it into a fixed state. The Shroud, which had never been seen, heard, touched, tasted, or smelled, was suddenly and violently incarnated into physical form. It was a man-shaped thing made of raw, red meat. Muscles without skin, twitching and spasming. Eyes blistered up from the wet tissue—dozens of them, on the chest, the back, the thighs, all unfocused and rolling. A mouth tore open in the throat, not to speak, but to scream, and the scream was the sound of a billion years of silence breaking.

Every sense it had never needed flooded in at once. The light of the chamber, gentle and ambient, was a searing lance through its new eyes. The sound of its own scream was a physical force that ruptured its eardrums instantly, only for the eardrums to regenerate so they could rupture again. The touch of the air on its raw nerves was an unending fire. It could smell its own meat, and the smell was panic. It thrashed on the marble floor, its untrained muscles tearing themselves from the bone, its thick blood pooling and slicking the stones.

Godric watched for perhaps four seconds, his expression one of mild, detached curiosity. Then he returned to his agricultural report. "It will stop when it runs out of nerves," he said.

It took seventeen hours. Seventeen hours of continuous, agonizing sensation before the Shroud's newly forged biology burned itself out. By the end, it had worn its own flesh down to a puddle of clear, odorless liquid. The floor was discolored for a week, despite the conceptual solvents.

There was no collateral damage this time. His perception was so precise, so perfectly aimed, that it touched nothing but the Shroud. That is, in its own way, more terrifying. He could have done it from across the omniverse. He could have smelled the Shroud before it even conceived its plan, could have perceived it into a screaming meat-thing eons ago. The only reason it existed long enough to enter his presence was because he found the faint trace of its ozone interesting.

I love my King. I know he is kind to me. He asked after my son's studies just yesterday. But when he walks past me, I hold my breath, and I flinch, because I know that his every sense is an apocalypse wrapped in a polite hello. Do not try to hide from him. You are already seen. You were seen before the first star ignited, and you will be seen after the last ember dies. The only mercy is that he usually chooses to look away.

I closed Orin's journal, my own senses feeling grossly inadequate and simultaneously overexposed. I could smell the dust of the Hall, the faint ozone of the stasis fields, and for a moment I imagined I could smell something else—the faint, watchful presence of the being whose notice was the most dangerous thing in existence. I shivered.

The next journal I pulled was old, the leather cracked with age, the handwriting an elegant, flowing script: *The Incident of Absolute Speed, Year 230,000, High Wizard Theron.*

**FROM THE JOURNAL OF HIGH WIZARD THERON — YEAR 230,000 — THE INCIDENT OF ABSOLUTE SPEED**

If time is a symphony, then what I witnessed today was the sound of the conductor's heart stopping mid-beat. I am a poet by inclination, a wizard by birthright, and a servant by love. What follows is my attempt to translate a horror so absolute that language itself curdles under the weight.

The Photovorous Swarm was a migratory feeding-cloud of temporal parasites. They were composed of pure velocity, born in the silent intervals between the ticks of the cosmic clock. To them, the speed of light was a stationary marker. They had devoured the chronal energy of entire multiverses, leaving behind frozen, dead timelines where nothing ever changed—where civilizations were trapped in eternal moments, their screams stretched into silent, endless chords. When they descended upon Thronegate, they did not attack. They began to nibble at our local timeflow, siphoning the river of seconds into their eternal, hungry acceleration.

Godric was in the Royal Observatory, standing before a newly restored tapestry depicting the fall of Olympus—a gift from my own great-grandfather's archive. He was tracing the thread of Queen Reina's golden hair with a fingertip, his expression soft with a grief that even a million years has not dulled. The first chronal eddy caused the candles to gutter. He did not raise his head. He did not activate a defense. He simply decided to shift his gaze from the tapestry to the window on the far side of the chamber.

The act required a rotation of his eyeballs. A mortal performs such an action in roughly a tenth of a second. Godric's speed has been multiplying by itself every Planck second for two hundred and thirty thousand years. By that date, his velocity was not a measure of fast; it was a measure of how far beyond the concept of "instant" one can travel while still being considered stationary. The infinitesimal fraction of 0% he permitted to express itself in that casual glance was, by any mathematical definition, still zero. And yet.

The friction of his irises rotating against the stationary medium of four-dimensional space-time ignited the temporal fabric. Time, as a dimension, caught fire. The Photovorous Swarm, existing entirely within the chronal current, experienced the shift as a sudden, infinite deceleration. They were not caught; they were anchored. Godric's glance had traversed its path faster than the minimum interval in which change can occur, which meant that the light of his motion arrived at the Swarm before the motion itself had begun. The resulting paradox—a cause arriving before its effect—shattered their velocity-based biology.

Each parasite was forcibly stretched across the entire history of the universe. Their bodies became Möbius strips of infinite agony, every segment of their existence experiencing the moment of being torn apart simultaneously. They did not die. Death would have been a release, and his speed did not offer releases. Instead, they were molecularized into the background radiation of the Big Bang, their screams becoming the hum of cosmic expansion. Their thrashing forms became the dark energy that pushes galaxies apart. They are not dead; they are a fundamental constant of nature now, their eternal, conscious torment an irrevocable law of physics.

The collateral damage was, in a word, absolute. Every universe that had ever been touched by the Swarm, and a decillion more that had not, experienced a temporal hiccup that reset their histories to random points. Civilizations were unborn and re-born. Entire species found themselves thrust backward into primordial ooze. It was a cataclysm of chronological chaos that would have been the end of all meaning. But Godric, his gaze now settled peacefully on the window, calmly exhaled. The chronal fires extinguished. The timelines straightened. The dead universes were restored to the exact picosecond before the incident, their denizens blissfully unaware that they had ever been unmade.

He did not restore the Swarm. They remain an eternal, agonized component of the universal constant, their suffering now a part of the mathematics that underpins all reality. Every time an astronomer measures the red shift, they are listening to the eternal scream of the Photovorous Swarm.

I am a man who has spent a lifetime seeking beauty. I have found it in music, in poetry, in the smile of my wife. But now I understand that the most terrifying beauty in the omniverse is the casual, effortless motion of my King's eyes. His mercy is the restoration of the canvas; his cruelty is the eternal torture of the paint. I will serve him until I die, and my poetry will forever carry the echo of those frozen screams, because that echo is now the rhythm of the cosmos.

I set Theron's journal down with hands that were, for the first time since my twentieth birthday, unsteady. A glance. A simple movement of the eyes. I thought of the countless times Godric had looked at me, his gaze always so warm, so present. How many invisible horrors had been passively avoided simply because he chose to look somewhere else? The tapestry of Olympus—he had been touching his dead wife's image, and with a casual shift of attention he had rewritten the laws of physics and condemned an entire species to eternal suffering. The grief and the cruelty coexisted, not as opposites, but as facets of the same infinite being.

I was trembling now, but I knew I had to finish. My father's final pointed gesture had been toward a slim, worn volume on a low shelf, its cover unadorned except for a name: *The Incident of Absolute Charisma, Year 850,000, Grand Magus Corvin.* I took it up like a holy relic—or a cursed one.

**FROM THE JOURNAL OF GRAND MAGUS CORVIN — YEAR 850,000 — THE INCIDENT OF ABSOLUTE CHARISMA**

They loved him. They loved him so much. I saw their hearts burst. I heard them laughing as they died. I am Corvin. I am not well.

The Iron-Willed Host was the most disciplined army ever assembled. Each soldier had undergone millennia of psychic conditioning to achieve a state of absolute emotional sterility. They were immune to telepathy, to mind control, to empathic suggestion, and to all forms of conceptual corruption. Their oath—carved into their very souls by rites that predated their universe—was singular: to destroy the Tyrant Godric. They did not seek wealth, power, or conquest. They sought only his death, and their will was an unbreakable diamond.

They gathered in the vast parade ground beneath the Spire of Eternity, their ranks stretching to a horizon that curved with the planet's surface. Ten trillion of the most willful beings in existence, their armor gleaming, their hearts silent. They did not scream threats. They simply stood, a patient, certain death.

Godric walked onto the balcony above them. He did not wear armor. He did not project an aura of fear. He did not speak a word. He simply stood there—a ruggedly handsome, forty-five-year-old man with salt-and-pepper hair and a neatly trimmed beard, his heavily muscled frame draped in simple dark fabrics. He looked at them.

And they saw him.

Not the Tyrant of propaganda. Not the monster that haunted their nightmares. They saw what we see: a father. A protector. A figure of such profound, bone-deep authority that every fiber of their conditioned being screamed "home." The infinitesimal fraction of charisma he allowed to touch them—a sliver of a sliver of 0% so small that no scale could register it—was not a command. It was an invitation. It was the unspoken promise of absolute, unconditional approval. The love of a parent who has infinite patience. The devotion of a god who has chosen you, specifically, to witness his glory.

The first ranks began to weep. Not tears of sorrow, but tears of overwhelming, ecstatic release. The emotional sterility that had been their greatest armor shattered like glass, and beneath it was a void that his charisma filled instantly. Generals who had not smiled in ten thousand years fell to their knees, their faces split by grins so wide their cheeks tore. They reached toward him, not to attack, but to touch, to be nearer.

The violence began not from Godric, but from them. Because his charisma is not a shared glory; it is a possessive one. Every soldier who beheld him wanted to be the sole recipient of that gaze, the only child in that father's eye. They turned on each other with their bare hands. They gouged out the eyes of their comrades so those eyes could not behold him. They bit through throats so that only their own voices could sing his praises. The parade ground became a pit of ecstatic slaughter, blood misting the air as bodies piled higher. They screamed his name—not "Godric," but "Father," "Love," "Mine."

The High Commander, a being of iron will who had once resisted the mental onslaught of a pantheon of psychic gods, tore off his own helmet and used the jagged edge to carve Godric's name into his chest. He was laughing as his heart pumped its last through the wound, his eyes locked on the balcony, his final breath a whispered "Thank you."

Godric watched. His expression was neutral, perhaps mildly interested. After a few minutes, he turned and walked back inside. The moment his gaze left them, a collective wail rose—a sound of such profound, soul-rending despair that the ground itself cracked. The survivors, unable to bear an existence without his attention, immediately set upon themselves with their weapons, their hands, their teeth. By nightfall, the entire Host, ten trillion of the most willful beings to ever exist, were a carpet of gore, their last moments spent in a frenzy of adoration.

He did not restore them. He never mentioned them again. To him, that glance was a fleeting moment of curiosity, a test of a stat he rarely bothered to examine. To them, it was the absolute purpose and culmination of their existence.

I felt the edges of that charisma. I was standing behind him, and even the reflected fraction of the fraction was enough to make me want to tear out my own heart and offer it to him. I resisted only because I already serve him, already love him as a thirsty man loves water. But the memory is a scar. I write this with a hand that still trembles, not from fear, but from the residual, aching need to please him, to be noticed by him.

This journal is a warning. Not against opposing him—that lesson is obvious. But against looking into his eyes when he is not accustomed to your presence. His love is a sun, and you are made of wax. Do not fly too close unless you are already his. And even then, bring a spare heart.

I closed Corvin's journal and sat in the profound silence of the Hall. The cognitive dissonance was a physical weight, a pressure behind my eyes that felt like the beginning of a scream. This was the man who had taught me to fish in the palace ponds. Who had laughed at my childish jokes and called me "little spark." Who had, just last week, asked me with genuine concern if I was sleeping well now that my Awakening was amplifying so rapidly.

And this was also the being who had, across a million years, effortlessly annihilated civilizations so completely that some had been redefined as natural laws, their suffering eternal and unremarked. The gap between these two truths was not a paradox; it was simply the reality of an existence so far beyond mine that my own consciousness was a single mote of dust floating in his infinite shadow.

I gathered the journals and returned them to their shelves. My legs carried me out of the Hall, through the corridor, and into the antechamber where my father waited. He looked at my face, saw the pallor of my skin, the tremor in my hands, and he simply nodded.

"Now you know," he said. "We serve him anyway. We love him anyway. Because he is our King, our friend, our protector. And because the alternative is not merely death; it is an irrevocable, eternal footnote in his casual passing attention."

I understood. The journals were not meant to inspire rebellion or despair. They were a vaccination against the arrogance of thinking that our own power, our amplified might, meant anything in the grand calculus. We were court wizards. We stood at his side not to shield him, but to remind ourselves, daily, that mercy was a gift and opposition was a mathematical impossibility.

Later that evening, I was summoned to the King's private study. Godric sat in a high-backed chair, a fire crackling in the hearth, the warm light dancing on his rugged features. He smiled when I entered, that familiar, heart-tugging smile.

"Kaedan," he said, his voice a rich, comforting baritone. "Come in, come in. I heard you've finished your mandatory reading. Dreadful business, I know. Your father always insists, though. He says it builds character."

I knelt before him, my head bowed. "Your Majesty."

He chuckled, a sound that filled the room with a sense of absolute safety. "Oh, stand up, boy. You're family. How many times must I tell you? Uncle Godric, remember?"

I rose, my eyes meeting his, and for a moment I saw only the warmth, the genuine affection that had defined my childhood. And then, behind that warmth, I saw the infinite, silent predator, the thing that could extinguish all of existence with a twitch, a glance, a thought. The terror and the love coiled together in my chest, inseparable.

"You look pale," he observed, his eyes crinkling with concern. "Did the journals upset you? I forget sometimes how... intense those old accounts can be. You must remember, Kaedan, that those were enemies who had chosen their path. I do not seek conflict. I merely conclude it."

I swallowed. "I know, Uncle Godric. I am not upset. I am... educated."

He smiled again, and the smile was sunset over the golden fields of a lost kingdom. "Good. Now, tell me about your plans for the new defensive wards. I hear you have some innovative ideas."

And so we talked. About wards, about runes, about the trivial details of court wizardry that he had no need for but always asked about anyway. He listened with genuine interest, nodding, asking questions. He was everything a king should be, and everything a god should not be. And I loved him for it, even as my soul screamed at the proximity.

Later, in my own chambers, I sat at my desk and opened a fresh journal. Its pages were blank, waiting for my first entry. I dipped the pen in ink and began to write, my words a record of this day, a warning for my own future son, and a testament to the truth I now held in my bones.

Opposing Godric is not merely fatal. It is a category error. Victory against him was impossible before the first atom sparked, and will remain impossible after the last. We serve him because his friendship is the only shore in an infinite ocean of his indifference. We love him because his love is the only law that protects us from his power.

And we fear him, every moment, because his power is the only thing that has ever, or will ever, exist.

**END**
